summary: Global equity markets are broadly risk-off as escalating US-Iran tensions dominate sentiment, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq declining, European shares edging lower, and Indian markets falling sharply amid surging oil prices. The primary macro catalyst is President Trump's reinstatement of an Iranian naval blockade and the imposition of a 20% charge on Strait of Hormuz cargo, a move that has slowed Hormuz traffic to a two-month low and sent oil prices surging over 4%, while Houthi strikes on Saudi Arabia's Sanaa airport and Iranian strikes on US bases have further inflamed the situation. Technology and semiconductor stocks are among the hardest-hit sectors, with chip stocks falling broadly on the risk-off move, though some analysts are treating the dip as a buying opportunity in select names; meanwhile, energy markets are tightening rapidly, with Europe's jet fuel stocks at critically low levels representing less than one month's supply. Investors are closely monitoring three key risks: a potential full-scale closure of the Strait of Hormuz disrupting roughly 20% of global oil flows, the inflationary feedback loop from higher oil prices reinforcing a higher-for-longer interest rate environment as reflected in rising bond yields and gold's counterintuitive decline, and currency stress in oil-importing economies like India where the rupee is under bearish pressure. In the short term, markets are likely to remain volatile and directionally driven by geopolitical developments in the Gulf, with any further military escalation or confirmed shipping disruptions poised to push oil higher, weigh on growth-sensitive assets, and keep central bank rate-cut expectations further suppressed.
